_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Burlington County, NJ. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Burlington was the colonial capital of West Jersey, and its records are among the oldest in New Jersey — deeds from the 1670s, Quaker meeting records from the founding generation, and Pine Barrens iron furnace records that document an entire vanished industry.
display_name: Burlington County, New Jersey — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Mount Holly, NJ — 49 Rancocas Road (with modern additions and annex buildings)

previous_courthouses.location: Burlington City — the colonial capital of West Jersey. Early courts met in various locations in Burlington before the county seat moved to Mount Holly.
previous_courthouses.fate: Burlington was the capital of West Jersey and the seat of government. When the county seat moved to Mount Holly in 1796, records were transferred. Some very early colonial records may have been lost or retained by the state.

record_losses.cause: No major courthouse fire. Some early colonial records have gaps due to age, handling, and the move from Burlington City to Mount Holly.
record_losses.description: Burlington County's records are among the most complete in New Jersey, reflecting its status as the colonial capital of West Jersey. Deed books survive from the county's founding in 1677. Will and estate records from the 1680s. No significant fire or flood has destroyed courthouse records. Some very early loose documents are fragile or damaged by age.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Burlington County at any point after 1677, the deed, will, and court records almost certainly survive. Gaps point to the ancestor's circumstances — Quaker families who transacted through meeting records rather than civil courts, or Pine Barrens furnace workers who left no property trail — rather than record loss.
record_losses.source: Burlington County Clerk's Office. Guide to Burlington County Records.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — among the oldest deed records in New Jersey
deeds.gaps: Some very early deeds are in fragile condition. West Jersey Proprietors' records supplement county deeds for the earliest period.
deeds.repository: Burlington County Clerk's Office, Mount Holly; NJ State Archives, Trenton
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; limited Ancestry.com NJ land records

wills_estates.completeness: Very good — continuous from the colonial era
wills_estates.gaps: Some early loose estate papers are fragmentary.
wills_estates.repository: Burlington County Surrogate's Office, Mount Holly; NJ State Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; NJ State Archives online catalog

marriages.completeness: Good from 1795 when NJ began requiring marriage licenses. Earlier marriages documented in Quaker meeting records and church records.
marriages.gaps: No civil marriage records before 1795. Quaker meeting records are the primary source for colonial-era marriages.
marriages.repository: Burlington County Clerk's Office; NJ State Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Very good — continuous from the West Jersey colonial era
court_minutes.gaps: Some early volumes in poor condition.
court_minutes.repository: Burlington County Clerk's Office; NJ State Archives

vital_records.completeness: NJ vital registration began in 1848 but compliance was spotty until approximately 1900. Burlington County compliance was moderate.
vital_records.repository: NJ State Archives; NJ Department of Health (post-1901)

substitute_sources.description: Burlington Monthly Meeting (established 1678) is one of the oldest Quaker meetings in America. Burlington Quarterly Meeting records cover the entire West Jersey Quaker community. Meeting records include births, marriages, deaths, removals, and disciplinary actions. Chesterfield, Crosswicks, and Evesham meetings also cover Burlington County territory.
substitute_sources.repository: Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College; Burlington County Historical Society; Haverford College Quaker Collection
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Exceptional. Burlington meeting records are among the most detailed in America. Removal certificates track migration. Marriage records document both families. Disciplinary minutes reveal personal details.
substitute_sources.source: Hinshaw, William Wade. Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy, Vol. 2. Ann Arbor: Edwards Brothers, 1938.

substitute_sources.description: The West Jersey Society and the Council of Proprietors of West Jersey maintained records of land purchases, surveys, and grants from 1676. These records predate and supplement county deed books for the earliest colonial period.
substitute_sources.repository: NJ State Archives; Burlington County Historical Society; Surveyor General's Office records
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for families arriving before 1700. Land grants document original settlers by name, acreage, and location.
substitute_sources.source: Pomfret, John E. The Province of West New Jersey. Princeton University Press, 1956.

substitute_sources.description: Batsto Iron Works (and later glass works), Atsion Furnace, and other Pine Barrens industrial sites maintained employee records, store ledgers, and production accounts. These records document the furnace workers — colliers, founders, teamsters — who left few traces in county civil records.
substitute_sources.repository: Batsto Village Historic Site (NJ Division of Parks); NJ State Archives; Burlington County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Pine Barrens families. Furnace store ledgers list purchases by name. Employment records identify workers. These sources are often the only documentation for Pine Barrens families who owned no property.
substitute_sources.source: Pierce, Arthur D. Iron in the Pines. Rutgers University Press, 1957.

narrative_hooks: Burlington County's records run from 1677 — the oldest in West Jersey. If {ancestor_name} lived in Burlington County at any point, the deed and will books almost certainly hold {pronoun_possessive} name.

For Quaker families like {ancestor_name}'s, the Burlington Monthly Meeting records are more detailed than anything the courthouse holds — births, marriages, removals, and disciplinary minutes from 1678.

If {ancestor_name} worked at Batsto Furnace, the county records may show nothing — but the furnace store ledger lists every purchase by name, and the employee records document a working life the courthouse never saw.
New Jersey required marriage licenses from 1795 — earlier than Pennsylvania (1885). If {ancestor_name} married in Burlington County after that date, the license survives.
source_references: Burlington County Clerk's Office. Guide to Burlington County Records. Mount Holly, NJ.
Hinshaw, William Wade. Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy, Vol. 2. Ann Arbor: Edwards Brothers, 1938.
Pomfret, John E. The Province of West New Jersey. Princeton: Princeton University Press, 1956.
Pierce, Arthur D. Iron in the Pines. New Brunswick: Rutgers University Press, 1957.
